title: Witch Week - Chapter 1 (Fragment 3)
text:
When he wrote woodwork second lesson, he meant that he had gone on to think about the second witch—which was a thing he did not think about so often. Woodwork was anything Charles liked. They only had woodwork once a week, and Charles had chosen that for his code on the very reasonable grounds that he was not likely to enjoy anything at Larwood House any oftener than that. Charles had liked the second witch. She had been quite young and rather pretty, in spite of her torn skirt and untidy hair. She had come scrambling across the wall at the end of the garden and stumbled down the rockery to the lawn, carrying her smart shoes in one hand. Charles had been nine years old then, and he was minding his little brother on the lawn. Luckily for the witch, his parents were out.

Charles knew she was a witch. She was out of breath and obviously frightened. He could hear the yells and police whistles in the house behind. Besides, who else but a witch would run away from the police in the middle of the afternoon in a tight skirt? But he made quite sure. He said, “Why are you running away in our garden?”

The witch rather desperately hopped on one foot. She had a large blister on the other foot, and both her stockings were laddered. “I’m a witch,” she panted. “Please help me, little boy!”

“Why can’t you magic yourself safe?” Charles asked.

“Because I can’t when I’m this frightened!” gasped the witch. “I tried, but it just went wrong! Please, little boy—sneak me out through your house and don’t say a word, and I’ll give you luck for the rest of your life. I promise.”

Charles looked at her in that intent way of his which most people found blank and nasty. He saw she was speaking the truth. He saw, too, that she understood the look as very few people seemed to. “Come in through the kitchen,” he said. And he led the witch, hobbling on her blister in her laddered stockings, through the kitchen and down the hall to the front door.

“Thanks,” she said. “You’re a love.” She smiled at him while she put her hair right in the hall mirror, and after she had done something to her skirt that may have been witchcraft to make it seem untorn again, she bent down and kissed Charles. “If I get away, I’ll bring you luck,” she said. Then she put her smart shoes on again and went away down the front garden, trying hard not to limp. At the front gate, she waved and smiled at Charles.

That was the end of the part Charles liked. That was why he wrote but not for long next. He never saw the witch again, or heard what had happened to her. He ordered his little brother never to say a word about her—and Graham obeyed, because he always did everything Charles said—and then he watched and waited for any sign of the witch or any sign of luck. None came. It was next to impossible for Charles to find out what might have happened to the witch, because there had been new laws since he glimpsed the first witch burning. There were no more public burnings. The bonfires were lit inside the walls of jails instead, and the radio would simply announce: “Two witches were burned this morning inside Holloway Jail.” Every time Charles heard this kind of announcement he thought it was his witch. It gave him a blunt, hurtful feeling inside. He thought of the way she had kissed him, and he was fairly sure it made you wicked too, to be kissed by a witch. He gave up expecting to be lucky. In fact,
to judge from the amount of bad luck he had had, he thought the witch must have been caught almost straightaway. For the blunt, hurtful feeling he had when the radio announced a burning made him refuse to do anything his parents told him to do. He just gave them his steady stare instead. And each time he stared, he knew they thought he was being nasty. They did not understand it the way the witch did. And, since Graham imitated everything Charles did, Charles’s parents very soon decided Charles was a problem child and leading Graham astray. They arranged for him to be sent to Larwood House, because it was quite near.

vocabulary.definition: a formal public statement.
vocabulary.example: ” Every time Charles heard this kind of announcement he thought it was his witch.

vocabulary.definition: to put things in order or make plans.
vocabulary.example: They arranged for him to be sent to Larwood House, because it was quite near.

vocabulary.definition: away from the correct path or direction.
vocabulary.example: And, since Graham imitated everything Charles did, Charles’s parents very soon decided Charles was a problem child and leading Graham astray.

vocabulary.definition: a bubble on the skin filled with fluid, caused by friction or injury.
vocabulary.example: She had a large blister on the other foot, and both her stockings were laddered.

vocabulary.definition: straightforward and brief, sometimes rudely so.
vocabulary.example: It gave him a blunt, hurtful feeling inside.

vocabulary.definition: a large controlled outdoor fire.
vocabulary.example: The bonfires were lit inside the walls of jails instead, and the radio would simply announce: “Two witches were burned this morning inside Holloway Jail.

vocabulary.definition: in a way showing great urgency or despair.
vocabulary.example:
”

The witch rather desperately hopped on one foot.

vocabulary.definition: feeling fear or anxiety; scared.
vocabulary.example: She was out of breath and obviously frightened.

vocabulary.definition: to walk with difficulty, typically because of pain or injury.
vocabulary.example: And he led the witch, hobbling on her blister in her laddered stockings, through the kitchen and down the hall to the front door.

vocabulary.definition: showing earnest and eager attention.
vocabulary.example:
”

Charles looked at her in that intent way of his which most people found blank and nasty.

vocabulary.definition: having runs or tears, especially in stockings or tights.
vocabulary.example: She had a large blister on the other foot, and both her stockings were laddered.

vocabulary.definition: to walk slowly and with difficulty because of injury or pain.
vocabulary.example: Then she put her smart shoes on again and went away down the front garden, trying hard not to limp.

vocabulary.definition: success or failure apparently brought by chance rather than one's own actions.
vocabulary.example: Please, little boy—sneak me out through your house and don’t say a word, and I’ll give you luck for the rest of your life.

vocabulary.definition: the power of making impossible things happen by using supernatural forces.
vocabulary.example:
”

“Why can’t you magic yourself safe?

vocabulary.definition: a reflective surface showing someone’s image.
vocabulary.example: ” She smiled at him while she put her hair right in the hall mirror, and after she had done something to her skirt that may have been witchcraft to make it seem untorn again, she bent down and kissed Charles.

vocabulary.definition: in a way that is easy to see or understand.
vocabulary.example: She was out of breath and obviously frightened.

vocabulary.definition: frequently; many times.
vocabulary.example: When he wrote woodwork second lesson, he meant that he had gone on to think about the second witch—which was a thing he did not think about so often.

vocabulary.definition: breathed quickly and heavily.
vocabulary.example: “I’m a witch,” she panted.

vocabulary.definition: to move quickly or clumsily using hands and feet.
vocabulary.example: She had come scrambling across the wall at the end of the garden and stumbled down the rockery to the lawn, carrying her smart shoes in one hand.

vocabulary.definition: to move quietly and secretly to avoid being noticed.
vocabulary.example: Please, little boy—sneak me out through your house and don’t say a word, and I’ll give you luck for the rest of your life.

vocabulary.definition: to look fixedly at someone or something.
vocabulary.example: He just gave them his steady stare instead.

vocabulary.definition: firmly fixed or constant.
vocabulary.example: He just gave them his steady stare instead.

vocabulary.definition: close-fitting coverings for the foot and part of the leg, typically worn by women.
vocabulary.example: She had a large blister on the other foot, and both her stockings were laddered.

vocabulary.definition: morally bad or evil.
vocabulary.example: He thought of the way she had kissed him, and he was fairly sure it made you wicked too, to be kissed by a witch.

vocabulary.definition: the practice of magical skills or abilities.
vocabulary.example: ” She smiled at him while she put her hair right in the hall mirror, and after she had done something to her skirt that may have been witchcraft to make it seem untorn again, she bent down and kissed Charles.
